1:
The chapter opens on the morning after Carmel, and the witnesses first register the shock of it: the great victory changed nothing at court. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry voices the disappointment for all — one would have expected that after fire and rain had both fallen from heaven at the prophet's prayer, all Israel would as one man return to the God of Israel and take Elijah for their guide; instead "he is neglected whom God honoured," and the land he had blessed is made too hot for him. Henry reads Ahab's report to Jezebel as deliberate incitement: his conscience would not let him persecute Elijah himself, so he told her "all that Elijah had done" — pointedly not what God had done, as if the prophet had by some charm brought down the fire — and dwelt on the slaying of Baal's prophets "to exasperate her," not to convince her.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the scene against Ahab's apostasy and read Jezebel's answering threat as impotentia muliebris iracundiae, the impotent rage of a woman who wants Elijah dead but dares not touch him — held back partly by the people, who were enthusiastic for the prophet, and partly by the king himself, on whom Carmel had not been without a salutary effect. The reformed John Gill supplies Ahab's own motive: he rehearsed the miracles — the fire that consumed even the stones and dust, the rain that came at Elijah's prayer — to "clear himself and make her easy," excusing his own helplessness before the crowd.

The witnesses divide over how much malice to read into Ahab's words. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke takes the charitable view — Ahab told Jezebel "probably with no evil design against Elijah," merely reporting the day's events — while Henry insists the telling was itself an act of cruelty, the work of those who, when they cannot for shame do mischief themselves, stir others to do it, and "will have it laid to their charge as if they had themselves done it." Beneath that difference the schools agree on the graver theme Henry draws out: the sudden collapse of Elijah's courage. He who had stood undaunted amid the terrors of heaven and earth now "trembles at the impotent menaces of a proud passionate woman." Gill turns it to the same lesson — the courage he had lately shown "was of the Lord, and not of himself" — so that the chapter's opening quietly prepares its whole burden: that great faith is not always alike strong, and that the man God had used against four hundred and fifty prophets is, of himself, no better than his fathers.
2:
Jezebel answers Carmel not with submission but with an oath, and the witnesses fasten on the strange fact that she warns her victim rather than striking him. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke renders the vow at its full weight: "If I do not slay thee, let the gods slay me with the most ignominious death" — a self-curse that measures the fury behind it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, tracing the Hebrew, see in the message the impotent wrath of a queen who cannot yet carry out her will: she confirms the threat by an oath after the manner of 1 Kings 2:23, swearing to make Elijah's life "as the life of one of them" by the same hour tomorrow.

Why, then, send word at all, and so give him time to flee? Here the witnesses offer complementary answers. The reformed John Gill weighs two possibilities — that she scorned to dispatch him privately and meant to make a public example, being "not as yet prepared for it," or, more deeply, that "there was an hand of Providence in it," so that the prophet might have time to make his escape. Keil presses the political calculation: she dared not seize him openly for fear of the people among whom he now had so great an interest. The reading Gill finally leans toward — and which the next verses bear out — is that a secret providence governed even Jezebel's rage, turning her boast into the very warning that preserved God's servant.

The verse thus stands as a hinge between triumph and flight. Its own words carry the menace: "So let the gods do to me, and more also." The witnesses do not soften it, but neither do they let it stand alone; they read it as the occasion God used, for the threat that was meant to end Elijah's work instead set him on the long road to Horeb, where the Lord would meet and re-commission him. That a heathen queen swears by gods who could do her no harm only sharpens the irony the commentators feel: the real power in the story is never hers, and the word she means for death becomes, under a hand she does not see, the means of life. What Jezebel intends as a boast of mastery is overruled to serve the very purpose she would destroy.
3:
The prophet who braved a king and four hundred and fifty priests now runs, and the witnesses read his flight as a study in the sudden failure of even the strongest faith. The reformed John Gill marks the reversal sharply: he "who had such courage as not to be afraid to meet Ahab... is now terrified at the threats of a single woman," which shows "that the spirit and courage he had before were of the Lord, and not of himself." Gill notes the variant the Septuagint, Vulgate, and Syriac preserve — "and he was afraid" — but says the sense stands either way: those with the greatest zeal, if left to themselves, become weak and timorous.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott turns the failure into a portrait of temperament. The "sudden reaction of disappointment and despondency," strange as it seems to superficial observation, is "eminently characteristic of an impulsive and vehement nature." Elijah's blow had been struck, as he thought, triumphantly; now the cold, unrelenting antagonism of Jezebel makes itself felt, "unshaken and only embittered by all that had passed," and, knowing he can rely neither on Ahab nor on the people, "once more he flees for his life." Ellicott finds significance in the note that Beersheba "belonged to Judah": the frontier town was in half-dependent alliance with Israel, and even under so good a king as Jehoshaphat Elijah might not think himself safe there — though his servant, traditionally the son of the widow of Zarephath, might stay without danger.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the flight as prudence overtaken by discouragement. Elijah "saw it was best to give place to this storm, and go to a place of safety"; he had probably thought the miracle at Carmel "would have been the means of effecting the conversion of the whole court," and, "finding himself mistaken, he is greatly discouraged." At the southern extremity of the promised land, under the jurisdiction of the king of Judah, he might suppose himself out of reach; and Clarke adds the tender detail that he left his servant behind not only to travel unnoticed but because "he did not wish to risk the life of his servant." The witnesses do not contend here: whether they weigh temperament, discouragement, or the withdrawal of divine strength, they agree that the collapse was real and instructive, and that the God who let his prophet run would follow him into the wilderness to restore him rather than abandon him to his fear.
4:
Alone in the wilderness under a solitary broom-tree, Elijah asks to die, and the witnesses gather around the pathos and the meaning of his prayer. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the emotional key: "in this disturbed state of mind he lay down and slept," and it is precisely here, at the point of despair, that "the Lord came with His power to the help of the despairing man." Charles Ellicott draws out what the complaint reveals. The exclamation "I am not better than my fathers" is "characteristic": Elijah had evidently hoped that he was better — "singled out beyond all those that went before him, to be the victorious champion of a great crisis, he, and he alone" — and now, his hope seemingly vain, he sees no reason why he should succeed when all before him failed, and asks why he should live when the rest of the prophets have died. Ellicott adds the naturalist's touch that the broom, "found abundantly in the desert," had roots much prized for charcoal — the very "coal" on which his bread is baked in v.6.

The reformed John Gill keeps to the geography, placing the day's journey some twenty miles south of Beersheba into the wilderness of Paran, the Arabian desert where Israel had wandered near forty years — a note that quietly links Elijah's coming forty-day pilgrimage to the nation's forty years. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke paraphrases the weary heart within the prayer: "I have lived long enough! I can do no more good among this people; let me now end my days." The juniper, he says, is simply "a tree that afforded him a shade from the scorching sun."

The witnesses do not dispute the facts; they interpret a mood, and what they will not do is flatter it. Elijah's request to die is read by all as the low ebb of a great soul — the same man who will never taste death at all, but be carried to heaven in a chariot of fire, here begs to be released from a life he thinks useless. The commentators let the contrast preach: the prophet's estimate of himself and God's estimate of him could hardly be further apart, and the God who "answered him not according to his folly" will meet his death-wish not with a grave but with bread, an angel, and a fresh commission. The despair is honest, and Scripture records it honestly; but it is the prelude to grace, not the refusal of it.
5:
God's first answer to the death-wish is not a word but a meal, and the witnesses dwell on the tenderness of it. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the sleep itself with sympathy: "excessive anguish of mind frequently induces sleep, as well as great fatigue of body," and into that exhausted slumber God sends relief — "he needed refreshment, and God sent an angel to bring him what was necessary." The reformed John Gill notes the small realism that Elijah slept "under a juniper tree," the same one under which he had sat, "for there was but one" in all that waste — a lone tree for a lonely man.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott weighs the word rendered "angel." It "may signify simply a messenger, human or super-human," but the context "suggests a miraculous ministration of some unearthly food." Ellicott then makes a striking observation about the silence of angels in this era: except as ministers of God in the physical sphere, the angels "whose appearances are so often recorded in earlier days, hardly appear during the prophetic period, as though the place of their spiritual ministry... had been supplied by the prophetic mission." Here the angel is "but auxiliary to the prophet, simply ministering to him in time of danger and distress" — and he draws the tender comparison that has moved readers ever since, "as the angel of the Agony to the Prophet of prophets."

The witnesses do not argue over this verse; they linger on it. The touch of the angel — gentle, wordless but for "Arise and eat" — is read as the very manner of God with his despairing servants. Elijah had asked to be left to die; heaven answers by refusing to leave him at all. That the same ministry which strengthened Elijah under the broom-tree would one day strengthen Christ in Gethsemane binds this desert scene to the deepest sympathy of God, who does not despise the weakness of those he has chosen, but stoops to feed the body before he corrects the soul. The commentators find in the sleeping prophet and the waking angel a picture of grace that meets us lower than our sin — at the very point of our wish to give up — and sets food before us there. What Elijah could not do for himself, and would not ask, is quietly done for him while he sleeps.
6:
The provision itself now comes into view — "a cake baken on the coals, and a cruse of water" — and the witnesses read in this plain fare the care of God for the body of his prophet. The reformed John Gill lingers on the manner of baking, arguing from Bochart that the cake was "baked on hot stones" after the eastern fashion, the stones perhaps "heated by a supernatural power, and the cake baked on them by an angel." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke agrees in a phrase: "all this seems to have been supernaturally provided."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch identify the dish precisely as a bread-cake baked over red-hot stones, "a savoury article of food which is still a great favourite with the Bedouins," and carry the narrative forward to the angel's second call and its reason — "for the way is too far for thee," a journey "greater than his strength." Charles Ellicott catches the human pathos in the closing clause, "and laid him down": there is "a pathetic touch in the description of the prophet, wearied and disheartened, as caring not to eat sufficiently, and glad, after a morsel eaten, to forget himself again in sleep."

Between these readings the verse gains both homeliness and depth. The witnesses agree that the food was miraculous, yet they refuse to make it grand: it is a poor man's cake and a jar of water, the fare of the desert, given not to indulge the prophet but to sustain him for a road he does not yet know he must travel. Ellicott's observation that Elijah ate too little and slept again shows a man still sunk in his grief, so that God must wake and feed him twice; and the commentators find here the patience of grace, which does not scold the half-hearted eater but comes "again the second time" with the same bread. The desert table set for one weary man becomes, in their hands, a parable of the Lord who knows our frame, remembers that we are dust, and strengthens us by degrees for burdens still hidden from our eyes. The cake on the coals is small, but the care behind it is boundless.
7:
The angel returns, and the witnesses treat this brief verse as the quiet doubling of God's mercy. The reformed John Gill notes only what the words say — that "the angel of the Lord came again the second time, and touched him," the touch given "in order to awake him out of sleep" — but the very brevity underlines the point: God will not let his exhausted servant lie there and perish. Once was not enough; the same messenger comes back, rouses him again with a touch, and calls him again to eat.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch supply the angel's reason, which is the heart of the verse: "Arise and eat, because the journey is too great for thee" — iter est majus quam pro viribus tuis, a way beyond his strength. The second meal is not indulgence but equipment. God, who alone knows the length of the road ahead, feeds his prophet for a journey Elijah has not yet been told to make, so that the strength is given before the demand is felt. Here the commentators see the settled pattern of providence: the God who appoints what the voyage shall be will victual the ship accordingly.

Read against its context, the verse gathers a weight it could not carry alone. Elijah had lain down under the broom-tree wishing to die (v.4); now he is being provisioned for forty days (v.8), a fast that will match Moses on the same mountain and, long after, the Lord in the wilderness. The double touch of the angel — twice waking, twice bidding him eat — mirrors the double provision by the ravens and the widow's cruse in the days before, so that the whole story of Elijah is punctuated by a God who feeds him at every extremity. The witnesses find in this small verse no controversy, only comfort: the same grace that meets us in our despair meets us a second time when once will not suffice, and strengthens us for burdens still out of sight. That God's care should be measured not by the prophet's request but by the prophet's need is the lesson all agree upon; Elijah asked to die, and instead was fed to live and to labour.
8:
Strengthened by that food, Elijah walks forty days and nights to Horeb, and the witnesses read the number as freighted with memory and the journey as a pilgrimage of the soul. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke states the parallel plainly: "he fasted just the same time as Moses did at Horeb, and as Christ did in the wilderness." The reformed John Gill keeps to the sober detail that he went in the strength "of what was left of the cake and cruse of water," the single meal carrying him across the whole desert.

The grammatical-historical witnesses probe the number more searchingly. Keil and Delitzsch observe that since the angel never told him where to go, Elijah's arrival at Horeb shows that a pilgrimage to "the mount of God" had been his own intention from the first: in the angel's strengthening he saw "an indication that he was to follow the footsteps of the divine grace still farther into the desert," hoping that there, as once to Moses, the Lord would reveal his counsel for the guidance of his people. Charles Ellicott presses the difficulty of the figure: forty days is "far in excess of what would be recorded for a journey of some two hundred miles," and so points rather to "an interval of retirement for rest and solitary meditation," like Moses' sojourn in Horeb and the Lord's in the wilderness (Exodus 24:18; Matthew 4:2), during which the prophet's spirit might be "calmed from the alternations of triumph and despondency." Through it all he went "in the strength" of the divine food, that he might learn that "man doth not live by bread alone, but by every word that proceedeth out of the mouth of God" (Deuteronomy 8:3).

The one open question among them — whether the forty days measure the march itself or a longer season of retreat — does not divide the witnesses on the substance. All hear the deliberate echo of Moses, and all see Elijah drawn, by a grace he does not fully understand, back to the mountain where the covenant was given. The commentators make the geography theological: the prophet who despaired of the law's cause is led to the very place of the law's giving, to be taught there that God's kingdom advances not by the fire he longs for but by word and Spirit. The forty-day fast marks him, with Moses and with Christ, as a man set apart for a fresh word from God.
9:
At the mount of God Elijah lodges in a cave, and the divine question "What doest thou here, Elijah?" sets the witnesses debating both the place and the tone. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch make much of the definite article — "the cave" — which points back to "the cleft in the rock" where Moses had stood while the glory of the Lord passed by (Exodus 33:22); and they read the question gently: it "did not involve a reproof, as though Elijah had nothing to do there, but was simply intended to lead him to give utterance to the thoughts and feelings of his heart." Charles Ellicott agrees it is "the cave," whether one already famous or one Elijah's sojourn would make famous, and holds that the word of the Lord came "in vision or dream at night," the Septuagint marking it by inserting "to-morrow."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry hears the question very differently — as a double reproof. He wonders what Elijah "proposed to himself" in coming so far: perhaps to indulge his melancholy, perhaps to strengthen his faith at the famous place where the law was given, hoping to meet God where Moses met him, or perhaps in token of abandoning a people who "hated to be reformed," echoing Jeremiah's wish for "a lodging place of wayfaring men" that he might leave his people (Jeremiah 9:2). To such hardship the apostle refers when he tells of saints who "wandered in deserts and in mountains, and in dens and caves of the earth" (Hebrews 11:38). For Henry the question rebukes both the fleeing and the fixing: "What, thou! so great a prophet — dost thou flee thy country?" — "Should such a man as I flee?" (Nehemiah 6:11) — and "What doest thou here, in this cave? Is this a place for a prophet of the Lord... when the public has such need of them?"

The reformed John Gill and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke stand between. Gill reports that the cave is shown to travellers still, at the foot of Sinai, and disputes the Jewish notion that it was the very cleft where Moses hid, since then "there would have been no need of Elijah to have gone forth" upon the mount. Clarke frames the question as an open probing: "Is this a reproach for having fled from the face of Jezebel, through... unbelieving fears?" The witnesses thus divide over the tone — Keil hearing an invitation to unburden the heart, Henry and Clarke hearing a rebuke — but they converge on the deeper point: God has followed his fugitive to the ends of the wilderness, for we cannot go anywhere to be out of the reach of God's eye, his arm, and his word, and the God who seeks Elijah out will not let him hide from either his grief or his calling.
10:
Elijah pours out his heart in a complaint the witnesses read with a mixture of sympathy and correction. The reformed John Gill takes the profession at face value: through "zeal for the glory of God" Elijah had slain the prophets of Baal, stirred Jezebel's revenge, and fled — a true account of a true jealousy. But the grammatical-historical witnesses hear more than zeal. Keil and Delitzsch find in the words "not only the greatest despair... but also a carnal zeal which would gladly have called down the immediate vengeance of the Almighty upon all idolaters," carrying a "tacit reproof that God had looked on quietly for so long," and an indirect appeal that he interpose at last. Charles Ellicott calls the reply "one of impatient self-exculpation and even remonstrance": Elijah had been very jealous for the Lord, "yet the Lord had not been jealous for Himself," suffering the people's rebellion and leaving "the one solitary champion left" unsupported and alone.

Ellicott sets the mood in a rich company of parallels. The complaint is "like that of Isaiah, 'O that thou wouldest rend the heavens and come down!'" (Isaiah 64:1); the zeal for God's glory, impatient of his long-suffering, is "like that of Jonah" (Jonah 4:1); and in "the Elias of the New Testament" the same despondent impatience breaks out in the message from prison, "Art Thou He that should come, or look we for another?" (Matthew 11:3). The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke lays out the four charges of the "very affecting" picture of apostate Israel: they have forsaken the covenant, thrown down the altars, slain the prophets, and now hunt the one prophet left.

Where the witnesses instruct us is in what they will and will not grant. All feel the genuine grief; none endorses the despair. The refrain "I, even I only, am left" is exposed by the very next revelation as mistaken — seven thousand still stood faithful — so that the complaint, sincere as it is, is also self-absorbed, the cry of a great man who has confused his own exhaustion with the failure of God's cause. The commentators let the tension stand: Elijah's zeal was real and God does not despise it, yet his verdict on the state of things was wrong, and the answer he receives will correct his numbers, redirect his energies, and, without ever scorning his sorrow, teach him that the kingdom does not rest on him alone.
11:
God answers the complaint first not with words but with a theophany, and the witnesses read the passing tempest, earthquake, and fire as a deliberate re-enactment of Sinai turned to a new lesson. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch say that "from the very earliest times" men have recognized here "a repetition of the appearance of God which was granted to Moses upon Sinai": as God once let his glory pass by Moses after his zeal for the Lord's honour, so now he displays his glory to "Elijah as a second Moses," for the strengthening of his faith. Charles Ellicott sets the vision between two earlier Horeb manifestations — the one to the people in "visible power, the whirlwind, the earthquake, and the fire" (Exodus 19:16), "the natural clothing of the terrors of the Law," and the one to Moses of "the Name of the Lord... merciful and gracious" (Exodus 34:5). Elijah's vision "disclaims the visible manifestation in power and vengeance, for which he had by implication craved," and reveals instead, in the "still small voice" — "the voice of a light breath" — the higher power of the Spirit that penetrates the inmost soul. Its lesson, Ellicott says, is simply, "Not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit, saith the Lord of hosts" (Zechariah 4:6).

The reformed John Gill and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke both preserve the Targum's reading of the phenomena as hosts of angels — "angels of wind," "of commotion," "of fire" — ministers who prepare the way of God's glorious Majesty. Gill draws the moral: God could as easily destroy the idolatrous kingdom as the wind rent the mountains and the fire consumed all in its way, "but he chose not to do it now, but to use lenity, and show mercy." Clarke canvasses the readings honestly — some take the sequence to teach that God comes "not in judgment, but in mercy," the terrors of wind and fire being forerunners of the gentle voice "which proclaimed the benignity of the Father of spirits," as the terrors of the law introduced the mild spirit of the gospel; others take it as a natural earthquake with its usual accompaniments, though Clarke judges "the exciting cause of the whole" to be supernatural.

For all their differences of detail, the witnesses land together. The God who could have answered Elijah's rage with the fire Elijah wanted instead teaches him that he was "not in" the fire. The prophet who came to Horeb craving vengeance is shown the manner of God's true working — hidden, gentle, effectual, the still small voice rather than the storm — and so is rebuked and consoled at once. The commentators make the whole scene a correction of Elijah's theology of power: the kingdom of God, like the mountain revelation itself, advances at last not by the earthquake but by the Spirit.
12:
The sequence completes itself — "and after the earthquake a fire... and after the fire a still small voice" — and with only two witnesses the reading concentrates on the contrast between the terrible and the gentle. The reformed John Gill notes that the Lord "was not in the fire" as he had been when he descended on this same mount to speak with Moses, and follows the Targum in taking the fire, like the wind and earthquake before it, as "an host of angels of fire" going before the Majesty of God. These emblems, Gill says, "represent the power of God, the terribleness of his majesty, and the fury of his wrath, which he could display, if he would, to the destruction of his enemies" — he could as easily consume the idolatrous kingdom as the fire consumes all in its way; "but he chose not to do it now, but to use lenity, and show mercy, signified by the next emblem."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the closing phrase with care as "a still, gentle rustling" — literally "the tone of a gentle blowing" — and use the participle "went by" to make the scene vivid. They stress that "tempest, earthquake, and fire... are signs of the coming of the Lord to judgment" (Psalms 18:8), the more terrible in "the awful solitude of the Horeb mountains"; and it is precisely by withholding himself from all three that God teaches his meaning. The Lord who could come in judgment comes instead in a whisper.

The two witnesses agree entirely. The point of the verse is not that God cannot wield the storm — he can, and the emblems prove it — but that at this hour he will not. Elijah, who had asked for the fire of vengeance, is answered by the gentle rustling, and in it learns that the God of Sinai is also the God of the still small voice: mighty enough for judgment, gracious enough to whisper. The commentators leave the prophet, and the reader, to feel the weight of that reversal, in which the loudest revelation proves the quietest, and mercy speaks where wrath might have thundered. What Elijah craved from heaven was fire; what heaven gave him was a voice, and in that voice the whole correction of his zeal.
13:
Elijah wraps his face in his mantle at the sound of the gentle voice, and the witnesses read the gesture as the fitting posture of a creature before its God. The reformed John Gill explains it doubly: "through reverence of the divine Majesty he perceived was there, and through shame and confusion under a sense of his impurity, imperfections, and unworthiness" — like the seraphim who cover themselves (Isaiah 6:2) and like Moses, who "hid his face" (Exodus 3:6). Gill notes the narrative detail that Elijah, checked earlier by the terrors of wind and fire, "now came quite out, and stood at the mouth of it, to hear what the Lord would say unto him."

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the veiling simply as respect: Elijah covered his face "to signify his respect; so Moses hid his face, for he dared not to look upon God," covering the face being "a token of respect among the Asiatics, as uncovering the head is among the Europeans." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch draw out the theology of the moment: while the appearance of God "not in the tempest, the earthquake, and the fire, but in a gentle rustling, revealed the Lord to him as a merciful and gracious God, long-suffering, and of great goodness and truth" (Exodus 34:6), the answer that follows showed "that He did not leave guilt unpunished" (Exodus 34:7), since the Lord at once gives him the sterner commission of the following verses.

The witnesses do not divide. Reverence, shame, and respect are not competing readings but facets of one act, and Keil's note binds the veiled face to the whole meaning of the theophany: the God revealed in the whisper is both merciful and just, and Elijah's covered face acknowledges both. The commentators hear the same repeated question, "What doest thou here, Elijah?", sounding a second time from the mouth of the cave, and with it the gentle insistence of God that his despairing servant speak his grief again — and hear again the answer that will send him back to work. The mantle over the face is the last image of Elijah's fear before the commission that ends it, the awe of a man who has met his God and is about to be sent afresh into the world.
14:
The question comes a second time, and Elijah gives, word for word, the same complaint — "I have been very jealous... and I, even I only, am left" — and the witnesses read the repetition and the divine response together. The reformed John Gill observes only that Elijah "says the same as before," the unchanged answer showing a grief not yet consoled. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that God's reply is designed so that the prophet "might learn, to his shame, that the cause of the Lord in Israel" was far less desperate than he had supposed — the very premise of his despair being false.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the fullest lesson from the exchange. When Elijah had wished to die, God "answered him not according to his folly," but now that he complains of discouragement — "and whither should God's prophets go with their complaints of that kind but to their Master?" — God gives him a real answer, and a threefold one: the wicked house of Ahab shall be rooted out, the apostate people shall be punished, and the prophet is neither alone nor without a successor, for a helper shall at once be raised up. Thus, Henry says, "all his complaints are answered and provided for." And to the lonely refrain "I only am left" God opposes the seven thousand hidden ones: "God's faithful ones are often his hidden ones" (Psalms 83:3), "and the visible church is scarcely to be seen." "The Lord knows them that are his, though we do not; he sees in secret."

The witnesses agree that the mercy of this verse lies in how God meets a repeated, self-pitying complaint — not with rebuke alone but with commission and comfort. Henry's tender application gathers it up: "When we come to heaven we shall miss many whom we thought to have met there; we shall meet many whom we little thought to have met there," for "God's love often proves larger than man's charity." The prophet who counted himself the last loyal man in Israel is about to learn that the God he served had reserves he had never seen, and work for him still to do. The second complaint, unchanged from the first, is answered not by indulgence but by a fresh call, and the despairing man is turned again toward his task.
15:
God's answer turns at once into marching orders: "Go, return on thy way to the wilderness of Damascus." The reformed John Gill reads it first literally — Elijah is to "go back the way he came, through the wilderness of Arabia" toward Damascus — but records also the figurative reading of Fortunatus Schacchus, that the phrase means "returning to his former course and custom of preaching the law of God, and reclaiming men from the error of their ways." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke notes the tender providence in the route: God "does not desire him to take a road by which he might be likely to meet Jezebel, or any other of his enemies." And Clarke supplies the reason for the whole commission of judgment: God "was about to bring his judgments upon the land, and especially on the house of Ahab," exterminating it by Jehu, while Hazael too would "grievously afflict Israel" (2 Kings 8:12).

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott hears in the single word "return" a pointed answer to Elijah's two complaints. The charge "conveys indirectly a double rebuke": Elijah's cry "I am not better than my fathers," which had implied that he "stood out beyond all others" to meet the crisis, is met by the command "to delegate the task of vengeance for God to others"; and his lament "I, even I alone, am left" is answered by "the revelation of the faithful remnant — the seven thousand who had not bowed to Baal — unknown to him, perhaps to one another, but known and loved by God."

The witnesses converge on the wisdom of the cure. Elijah's despair had two roots — an inflated sense of his own indispensability and a mistaken sense of his solitude — and God heals both at once by giving him work that will outlive him and colleagues he never knew he had. The very act of anointing others to carry the cause forward is the antidote to a prophet's belief that all depends on him. Instead of the fire he wanted from heaven, Elijah is handed a commission to set in motion the human instruments — a foreign king, a native king, and a successor prophet — through whom God will accomplish, in his own way and time, the judgment and the preservation of his people. The word to the despairing man is not "rest" but "return," and in the returning lies his healing.
16:
The commission widens to name Jehu and Elisha, and the witnesses fasten on the puzzle of an "anointing" the narrative never records. The reformed John Gill notes that Jehu "does not appear to have been anointed by Elijah, but by Elisha," yet, "being done by his order and direction," it was "the same as if it was done by himself." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the point into an argument for the history's authenticity: Elijah "fulfilled in person but one part" of the threefold charge — the call of Elisha — the other two being carried out later by Elisha and his agents (2 Kings 8:8; 2 Kings 9:1); and this "apparently imperfect correspondence of the event to the charge is a strong indication of the historical character of the narrative." Ellicott observes that no actual anointing of Elisha is anywhere recorded, and traces the rite from the priests (Exodus 40:15; Numbers 3:3) to the kings, leaving open "whether the prophets were always consecrated with the sacred oil" or whether the call itself sufficed.

This is the verse's genuine debate: was Elisha literally anointed, or is "anoint" here a figure for the prophetic call? Ellicott holds the question open; Gill supposes either a real anointing by Elisha or that Elisha was "twice anointed"; and Clarke will conclude — when he reaches the mantle cast on Elisha — that "anoint" signifies "no more than the call to the office, accompanied by the simple rite of... the prophet's mantle thrown over his shoulders."

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke uses the verse to demolish a rabbinic gloss. Jarchi read the command to appoint Elisha "in thy room" as God's rebuke of Elijah — "thy prophecy... does not please me, because thou art the constant accuser of my children" — as though those "vile idolaters and murderers were still the beloved children of God" on the principle "once in the covenant, always in the covenant." Clarke answers with the testimony of the prophet and of history itself: the people "had forsaken the covenant, and consequently renounced all their interest in it." The witnesses thus agree on substance while differing on ceremony: whether by oil or by mantle, God is appointing the successors through whom his purpose will run on when Elijah is gone, and the covenant's blessings belong not to birth but to faithfulness.
17:
The three instruments are set in a chain of judgment — "him that escapeth the sword of Hazael shall Jehu slay, and him that escapeth from the sword of Jehu shall Elisha slay" — and the witnesses puzzle over how the peaceable Elisha can be said to "slay" at all. The reformed John Gill takes the plain sense that "many should be slain in Israel by them both," Hazael and Jehu, the survivors of the one falling to the other. But the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke observes at once that "we do not find that Elisha either used the sword, or commissioned it to be used," and offers the resolution: Hazael, Jehu, and Elisha are together "the ministers of my vengeance against this disobedient and rebellious people," while "the order of time, here, is not to be regarded" — indeed Elisha was prophet before Hazael was king. Elisha's slaying, then, is the sword of prophecy, not of steel.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott makes the same difficulty a further proof of the narrative's truth. The vengeance of Hazael and Jehu "speaks for itself," written "in bloody letters on the history"; but "Elisha's mission was obviously not one of such vengeance" — "he had to destroy enmity, but not to slay the enemies of God." That the charge is framed in terms Elisha's actual ministry would not literally fulfil is "one of the many marks of historic accuracy," for the words are cast "in the terms in which Elijah would best understand it." "His spirit was for war; he could hardly have conceived how the completion of his mission was to be wrought out by the weapons of peace in the hand of his successor" (2 Corinthians 10:3).

The witnesses do not so much divide as together loosen a knot. All agree the verse cannot mean that Elisha wielded a literal sword; all agree that the three names together spell the certainty of God's judgment on an apostate kingdom; and all sense the quiet irony Ellicott names — that the prophet whose zeal ran to fire and sword is promised a successor whose "weapons of peace" would accomplish what his violence could not. The threefold sword is God's assurance to a discouraged man that his cause will not fail for want of instruments: when Hazael's war and Jehu's justice have done their work, the word in Elisha's mouth will finish it.
18:
The chapter's great comfort arrives — "Yet I have left me seven thousand in Israel, all the knees which have not bowed unto Baal" — and the witnesses read it as the direct correction of Elijah's "I only am left." The reformed John Gill ties it to the preceding judgments: these seven thousand are reserved "from perishing by the sword of either of them," Hazael or Jehu — a faithful remnant kept through the very chastisement that falls on the nation. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the number as "a number of perfection," standing for "many thousands": though "the court and multitudes of the people had gone after Baal," perhaps "the majority of the common people still worshipped in secret the God of their fathers." Clarke then unfolds the idiom "every mouth which hath not kissed him," explaining the ancient act of adoration — kissing the hand toward, or the image of, the idol — and even the etymology of "adore" (ad, to, and os, the mouth), with instances from Pliny and Cicero of statues worn away by worshippers' kisses.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott corrects the tense — it should be "I leave," or "will leave," "through all this vengeance, the seven thousand faithful" — and sets them among "the faithful remnant sealed in the visions of Ezekiel and St. John in the day of God's judgment" (Ezekiel 9:4; Revelation 7:3). The kissing, he notes, describes the worshipper "bowing the knee" and then "kissing the image, or the altar, or the threshold" (Job 31:26; Hosea 13:2).

Beneath the philology the witnesses hold one great truth, which the apostle would later seize upon: in the darkest apostasy God always keeps a remnant "according to the election of grace" (Romans 11:4). Elijah could not see them — faithfulness in that day was largely hidden, worshipping in secret — but God both knew and had preserved them, for the emphatic "I have left me" makes their fidelity his work, not merely their own. The verse thus rebukes the prophet's loneliness at its root: he was never the last, and the cause he thought dead was quietly alive in thousands of unbowed knees. What looked to Elijah like total defeat was, in God's account, a sifted but surviving church.
19:
Elijah finds his successor at the plough, and the witnesses turn the scene into a meditation on how God calls his servants. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fix the picture: Elisha was ploughing "with twelve yoke of oxen before him, and he himself with the twelfth" — "a very wealthy man therefore" — when Elijah "threw his cloak to him as he passed by," the prophet's cloak being "the sign of the prophet's vocation, so that throwing it to him was a symbol of the call to the prophetic office." Charles Ellicott agrees the twelve yoke "indicate some wealth," which Elisha "has to leave to follow the wandering life of Elijah," and marks the contrast between the two men — "the quiet simplicity of Elisha's call" against "the sudden, mysterious appearance of Elijah"; the mantle, "the rough hair-mantle characteristic of the ascetic recluse," is cast in an act "said to have been a part of the form of adoption of a child," hence its spiritual force.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws out the homely dignity of the call. Elisha was named last in God's orders but "was first called," and Elijah, "far from being jealous of his successor," rejoices to leave God's work "in such good hands." He was found "not in the schools of the prophets, but in the field... not reading, nor praying, nor sacrificing, but ploughing," for "idleness is no man's honour, nor is husbandry any man's disgrace"; an honest calling "does not at all put us out of the way of our heavenly calling," and Elisha was "taken from following the plough to feed Israel," as the apostles were "taken from fishing to catch men." The casting of the mantle was "a powerful call": "an invisible hand touched his heart," for "it is in a day of power that Christ's subjects are made willing" (Psalms 110:3).

The reformed John Gill and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fill in the rest. Gill measures the long road from Horeb to Abelmeholah; Clarke reckons that a man keeping "twelve yoke of oxen" had "a considerable estate," so that his obedience came "at considerable secular loss," and identifies the mantle as "the peculiar garb" of the prophet (Zechariah 13:4; 2 Kings 1:8), "probably made of skin dressed with the hair on." The witnesses do not contend; together they make Elisha's call a study in grace and vocation — the God who had just comforted Elijah with the seven thousand now gives him the one man who will carry his mantle, and the busy farmer, touched by an unseen power, leaves his oxen to follow. The lonely prophet is given a companion, and the work its next hands.
20:
Elisha runs after the departing prophet asking leave to kiss his father and mother, and the witnesses weigh both his request and Elijah's curious reply, "Go back again; for what have I done to thee?" The reformed John Gill grounds the whole in grace: Elisha left the oxen and ran, "his heart being touched by the Lord at the same time, and his mind enlightened to understand what was meant by that action" of the mantle. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read Elijah's words as a release, not a rebuff: "Go back again... what have I done to thee?" can "only mean, I have not wanted to put any constraint" upon you — Elisha is free to take his leave. They add the fine allusion that as Elisha had ploughed his earthly field "with his twelve pair of oxen," he was now "to plough the spiritual field of the twelve tribes of Israel" (Luke 9:62).

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke is sure Elisha "fully understood that he was called... to the prophetic office," and "conferred not with flesh and blood, but resolved, immediately... to obey; only he wished to bid farewell to his relatives." Elijah's answer he glosses, "Thy call is not from me, but from God: to him... art thou accountable." Charles Ellicott compares the scene with the would-be disciple in the Gospel (Luke 9:61) and reads Elijah's reply as "half-ironical rebuke of what seemed hesitation — 'Go back, if thou wilt; what have I done to constrain thee?'" — "the stern but necessary rejection of half-hearted service," though "Elijah sees that Elisha means simply farewell, and he apparently waits till it is over."

Here the witnesses differ in shading. Keil and Clarke take the reply as pure liberty — no constraint laid, so go freely; your call is from God, not from me — while Ellicott hears in it a probing edge that tests whether the young man's heart is divided, as the Lord's harder word tested another who looked back from the plough. Both anchor the moment in the Gospel parallel, and both agree that Elisha passes the test: his was no excuse for delay but only the honouring of natural affection. The commentators leave the scene poised between the claims of home and the claim of God, and find the call vindicated — Elisha will kiss his parents and then follow, his obedience prompt, his heart undivided, the mantle already doing its secret work.
21:
Elisha's call is sealed with a farewell feast and a decisive following, and the witnesses read the killing of the oxen as an act of full surrender. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch describe it exactly: Elisha "took the pair of oxen with which he had been ploughing," slaughtered them, "boiled the flesh with the plough" — the wooden gear serving as fuel — "gave a farewell meal to the people" of his place, "and then followed Elijah as his servant." Charles Ellicott hears the finality in it: like Matthew leaving the receipt of custom, Elisha "makes a feast of farewell to his home, and of homage to his new master," the hasty fire kindled from the wooden implements as at Araunah's threshing-floor (2 Samuel 24:22); "henceforth from a master he became a servant," content to be known ever after as "he that poured water on the hands of Elijah" (2 Kings 3:11).

The reformed John Gill keeps the sequence — Elisha "returned back," took his leave at his father's house, and came again into the field. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the burnt plough as symbol: he "boiled the flesh of the oxen with his agricultural implements, probably in token that he had abandoned secular life." Clarke draws from the call three lessons — that "anoint" here means "no more than the call to the office... the prophet's mantle thrown over his shoulders"; that "a call to the ministerial office... never supersedes the duties of filial affection"; and that Elisha's parents, having "too much respect for the authority of God," made no attempt to hinder him, unlike those who "for filthy lucre's sake" would keep a son from the ministry.

The witnesses do not divide; they gather the chapter to its quiet close. The prophet who at its opening fled alone into the wilderness, wishing to die, ends it with a companion and a successor at his side — the seven thousand still unbowed, the kings of judgment named, and now the young ploughman following in the mantle. Elisha's slain oxen and burning plough answer, from the human side, to the total claim of the call: he leaves wealth, home, and settled life to "minister" to another, and in that willing service begins the prophetic office he will one day fill. The commentators find here the pattern of every true following — counting the cost, taking leave, and then, without looking back, going after the God who calls.
